citation: Cosio PL, Moreno-Simonet L, Fernandez D, Lloret M, Padulles X, Padulles JM, Farran-Codina A, Rodas G, Cadefau JA. Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. European Journal of Applied Physiology. 2024;(online ahead of print).
abstract: This repeated-measures study tested whether early hamstring rate of torque development (RTD) can detect residual fatigue after a soccer match. Nineteen male regional first-division soccer players completed a hamstring-specific 90 degree hip:20 degree knee isometric posterior chain test before match day and at 24, 48, and 72 hours post-match, alongside serum biomarkers of muscle damage, mitochondrial damage, oxidative stress, and recovery. Match load averaged 10,463 m total distance, 466 m high-speed running, 45 accelerations over 3 m/s2, and 59 decelerations over 3 m/s2. Early RTD at 0-50 ms and 0-100 ms remained depressed through 72 hours in both limbs, while maximal voluntary isometric contraction torque was depressed through 48 hours but recovered by 72 hours. CK, CK/ALT, CK/AST, sMtCK, MDA, and irisin remained elevated through 72 hours, supporting persistent neuromuscular and cellular recovery demands.
tldr: Early hamstring RTD stayed suppressed for at least 72 hours after match play even after peak isometric torque recovered, making short-window RTD a more sensitive readiness marker during congested schedules.
methods: Repeated-measures recovery study in 19 male outfield soccer players; baseline testing before an experimental match and retesting at 24, 48, and 72 hours; 90 degree hip:20 degree knee isometric posterior chain test with three 3-s MVIC trials per limb; outcomes included MVIC torque, RTD0-50, RTD0-100, RTD0-250, RTDpeak, and RTD normalized to MVIC; blood sampling quantified CK, ALT, AST, sarcomeric mitochondrial CK, malondialdehyde, and irisin; match running load was captured with wearable tracking; repeated-measures statistics were run in SPSS v27.
findings:
- RTD0-50 and RTD0-100 were significantly reduced in both hamstrings through MD+3, with large effects reported in the abstract (50 ms p<0.001, g=-1.24; 100 ms p<0.001, g=-1.06).
- MVIC torque was reduced through MD+2 in dominant and non-dominant limbs but recovered by MD+3.
- Normalized RTD showed persistent reductions at 0-50 ms and 0-100 ms through 72 hours, while normalized RTD0-250 and RTDpeak did not significantly change.
- RTD testing reliability was moderate to good and MVIC reliability was excellent (MVIC ICC=0.987).
- CK activity, CK/ALT, CK/AST, sMtCK, MDA, and irisin remained elevated through MD+3, linking RTD deficits to structural, mitochondrial, and oxidative stress markers.
- No meaningful between-limb differences were found in fatigue kinetics.
limitations: Small single-team male soccer sample; experimental match setting may not capture full competitive, tactical, travel, and psychological stress; only a 72-hour recovery window was observed; no direct hamstring injury outcome was measured; biomarkers are systemic and cannot isolate hamstring tissue specifically; findings are from association football rather than American football.
practicalImplications: For football sport science staff, rapid-force hamstring testing may reveal residual fatigue that peak strength alone misses. A short isometric pull in a hip-flexed/knee-extended position could be used after high-speed or game-like exposures to guide sprint volume, high-speed exposures, and posterior-chain loading before the next hard practice. Peak force returning to baseline should not automatically mean the athlete is ready for maximal sprinting if early RTD remains suppressed.
athleteDev: This supports building routine hamstring rapid-force profiles for WR, DB, RB, LB, and specialists exposed to frequent sprinting. Training should develop both maximal eccentric strength and the ability to express force quickly in long hamstring lengths, while monitoring whether athletes recover their short-window force production across weekly practice cycles.
rtp: Not an RTP intervention study, but directly relevant to hamstring return-to-speed decisions. Before full-speed reintegration after hamstring injury or heavy sprint exposure, staff should consider early RTD recovery in addition to pain, ROM, peak force, sprint mechanics, and GPS progression. The study suggests a 72-hour window may still be insufficient for full hamstring neuromuscular recovery after match-level high-speed work.

citation: de Oliveira DM, Silva AKAC, Macedo AG, Fernandes MB, Fernandes EV. Global Research Trends in Sports Nutrition and Football over the Last 20 Years (2004-2024). Sports. 2025;13(10):365.
abstract: This scientometric review mapped research on sports nutrition applied to soccer from 2004 through 2024. Searches in Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus returned 2636 documents; after excluding reviews, meta-analyses, duplicates, and out-of-scope football codes, 526 original field-soccer articles were analyzed with Bibliometrix and VOSviewer. Publications increased from 4 in 2004 to 62 in 2024, a 1450% rise, with an annual growth rate of 14.6%. Nutrients was the leading source, Morton J and Maughan R were the most productive authors, Liverpool John Moores University was a major collaboration hub, and the United Kingdom led production and citations. Earlier work focused on hydration and dietary optimization, while newer themes include low energy availability, polyphenols, anthropometry, supplementation, recovery, oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, beta-alanine, and antioxidant markers.
tldr: Soccer nutrition research has expanded sharply since 2004, shifting from hydration-heavy work toward individualized fueling, energy availability, supplementation, recovery, and oxidative-stress questions.
methods: Scientometric review of original articles published January 2004 to December 2024; databases searched in March 2025 were Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus; search terms combined nutrition status, dietary intake, sports nutrition, macronutrients, micronutrients, energy availability, hydration, and supplementation with soccer/football terms; reviews, meta-analyses, duplicates, futsal, rugby, American football, Australian football, Gaelic football, referees, adapted soccer, and non-scope sports were excluded; 526 articles were analyzed with Bibliometrix in R and VOSviewer for annual production, authors, journals, citations, countries, collaborations, keywords, conceptual structure, and co-occurrence networks.
findings:
- Final sample included 526 original articles in 197 journals with 2478 authors and 23.9% international collaboration.
- Publication output rose from 4 articles in 2004 to 62 in 2024, with strongest growth from 2020 onward.
- The United Kingdom, Brazil, Spain, and the United States were the most productive countries; the United Kingdom accounted for the largest citation share among the top cited countries.
- Nutrients was the leading publication source; Morton J and Maughan R were the most productive authors.
- Early themes centered on hydration, thermoregulation, sports drinks, electrolytes, and fluid balance.
- Recent/emerging themes include low energy availability, polyphenols, body composition, vitamin D, supplementation, recovery, oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, beta-alanine, and antioxidant markers.
limitations: Scientometric mapping identifies publication patterns, not clinical effectiveness. Results depend on database coverage, search terms, metadata quality, citation lag, and English/indexed literature visibility. Exclusion of reviews and other football codes narrows scope. The paper is soccer-focused, so translation to American football requires practitioner judgment around body size, collision load, energy expenditure, and season structure.
practicalImplications: The review highlights where nutrition support is moving: individualized fueling, low energy availability screening, body composition monitoring, recovery nutrition, polyphenol-rich foods/supplements, and selected ergogenic aids. For Baylor football, the actionable takeaway is to keep nutrition surveillance tied to training load and recovery markers rather than treating hydration or supplementation as standalone checkboxes.
athleteDev: Although from soccer, the research trends map well to football development priorities: maintain energy availability through camp and heavy lifting blocks, individualize carbohydrate and protein targets by position and body-composition goal, monitor vitamin D and micronutrient risk, and evaluate supplementation through performance/recovery outcomes rather than popularity.
rtp: No direct RTP data were analyzed. Indirectly, the emerging focus on low energy availability, polyphenols, oxidative stress, and recovery nutrition supports integrating dietitian-led fueling and recovery plans into injury rehabilitation, especially during high-volume return-to-running or return-to-contact phases.

citation: Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the National Football League: An epidemiological study. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2022;(in press).
abstract: This epidemiological study analyzed in-game hamstring injuries from official NFL gamebooks during the 2013-2016 regular seasons. Seventy-eight qualifying hamstring injuries were identified and cross-referenced with FoxSports.com. Standardized incidence ratios were calculated by position group using expected play exposure, and injury distributions were compared by field surface, short-rest weeks, and week of season. Linebackers and defensive backs had disproportionately high hamstring injury incidence (SIR 2.02 and 1.62, respectively), while offensive and defensive linemen had lower-than-expected incidence (SIR 0.28 and 0.29). Turf accounted for 57.7% of hamstring injuries versus 43.75% of games, but this was not statistically significant. Short rest and week of season were not significant predictors, although the mean hamstring injury week was 8.05.
tldr: NFL hamstring injury risk was highest in linebackers and defensive backs, while turf and four-day rest did not reach statistical significance in this 2013-2016 in-game sample.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological analysis of publicly available official NFL gamebooks from 2013-2016 regular-season games; included all in-game hamstring strains, partial tears, and complete tears; practice, offseason, and non-game injuries were excluded; injuries were cross-referenced with FoxSports.com; position groups were QB, RB, WR, TE, OL, DL, LB, and DB; expected exposure was estimated from offensive 2021 and defensive 2020 personnel usage; field surface was categorized as grass or turf; short rest was defined as four days; analyses used SIRs, two-proportion z tests, chi-square tests, and unpaired t tests in Microsoft Excel.
findings:
- 78 in-game hamstring injuries were included; 8 injuries (10.3%) occurred in players with a prior in-game hamstring injury during the study period.
- Linebackers had elevated relative incidence (SIR 2.02; 95% CI 1.14-2.91), and defensive backs also exceeded expectation (SIR 1.62; 95% CI 1.14-2.10).
- Offensive linemen and defensive linemen had significantly lower-than-expected hamstring injury proportions (SIRs 0.28 and 0.29).
- 45 of 78 hamstring injuries (57.7%) occurred on turf and 33 (42.3%) on grass; turf did not differ significantly from game distribution (p=0.082) and field type was not associated with injury type (p=0.283).
- Five hamstring injuries (6.41%) occurred on four-day rest weeks, nearly identical to the 6.25% of games on short rest (p=0.959).
- Mean week of hamstring injury was 8.05 (95% CI 7.06-9.04), not significantly different from other injuries (mean 8.84; p=0.159).
limitations: Gamebooks do not capture practice, offseason, preseason, playoff, or college injury histories; prior hamstring injury and recurrence are likely undercounted; injury mechanism and severity were unavailable; expected positional exposure came from 2020-2021 personnel data rather than the 2013-2016 injury seasons; public reporting may misclassify or omit details.
practicalImplications: Hamstring prevention and monitoring should be especially focused on linebackers and defensive backs because their roles demand repeated start-stop, acceleration, backpedal, transition, and change-of-direction actions. Surface and short-rest results should not be overinterpreted as 'no risk,' but this dataset suggests position-specific sprint/COD exposure is a stronger signal than turf or Thursday-style scheduling.
athleteDev: For Baylor football, DB and LB development should include progressive high-speed exposure, deceleration capacity, eccentric hamstring strength, posterior-chain asymmetry monitoring, and position-specific transition drills. Linemen still need hamstring robustness, but their lower relative incidence suggests different loading profiles and prevention priorities.
rtp: The study reinforces caution with early RTP after hamstring injury: cited NFL literature in the discussion notes recurrence risk and a higher reinjury risk when players return within two weeks. RTP should require position-specific sprinting, acceleration/deceleration, and COD tolerance for DBs and LBs rather than clearance based only on time and symptoms.

citation: Valente HG, Oliveira RR, Baroni BM. How are hamstring strain injuries managed in elite men's football clubs? A survey with 62 Brazilian physical therapists. Physical Therapy in Sport. 2023;61:73-81.
abstract: This cross-sectional survey described hamstring strain injury assessment, rehabilitation, and return-to-play practices among physical therapists working in elite Brazilian men's soccer. Sixty-two physical therapists from 35 of 40 eligible first- and second-division clubs participated, representing 87.5% of clubs. All respondents reported using imaging exams, injury classification systems, and assessments of pain, range of motion, strength, and functional status. Rehabilitation was commonly organized into 3-4 phases. Electrophysical agents and stretching were used by all respondents; 98.4% used strengthening exercises, 93.5% included eccentric overload, 96.8% used manual therapy, 95.2% used football-specific functional exercises, and 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization. Muscle strength was the most commonly reported RTP criterion, selected by 71% of respondents.
tldr: Elite Brazilian soccer PTs consistently use imaging, classification, strength/ROM/pain/function assessment, phased rehab, eccentric strengthening, lumbopelvic work, and football-specific drills, but objective strength/ROM and RTP criteria remain variable.
methods: Cross-sectional online survey of physical therapists from Brazilian men's Serie A and Serie B clubs in the 2021 season; questionnaire created by experienced football PT/researcher authors and reviewed by two external elite-club PTs; final Portuguese questionnaire had 30 questions across club structure, assessment, and rehabilitation practices; invitations and follow-up were sent by WhatsApp/email; data collection occurred March-December 2021; descriptive results were reported as counts and percentages.
findings:
- 62 PTs from 35 of 40 eligible clubs participated, providing 87.5% club representativeness.
- All respondents used imaging and classification systems; 74.2% rated imaging very important and 25.8% important.
- Munich Consensus classification was used by 45.2%, British classification by 24.2%, subjective mild/moderate/severe by 24.2%, and grade I-III by 19.3%.
- 82.3% of clubs had standard assessment protocols, 72.6% had standard rehab protocols, and 88.7% had standard RTP protocols.
- Rehab was organized in phases by 90.3%; most used 3 phases (45.2%) or 4 phases (33.9%).
- Common rehab elements included electrophysical agents (100%), ROM work/stretching (100%), strengthening (98.4%), eccentric overload (93.5%), manual therapy (96.8%), lumbopelvic stabilization (93.5%), and football-specific demands such as accelerations, high-speed turns, high-speed running, kicking, jumps, and sprints (88.7%-95.2%).
- RTP release criteria were most often muscle strength (71%), sprints/high-speed running (58.1%), intense actions/accelerations (43.5%), pain (43.5%), GPS workload (32.3%), ROM (27.4%), functional tests (27.4%), and imaging (22.6%).
limitations: Brazil-only sample may not generalize to other countries or women's football; cultural, education, and club-resource differences may shape practice patterns; self-completed questionnaire data can be inconsistent or socially desirable; the study describes practice frequency, not which practices improve outcomes or reduce recurrence.
practicalImplications: The survey provides a useful benchmark for a high-resource football rehab model: combine objective diagnosis/classification, phased progressions, eccentric strength, lumbopelvic control, sprint/COD progression, and periodic reassessment. A key gap for staff is standardizing objective criteria, since many practitioners still rely heavily on subjective ROM or manual strength testing despite recommendations for quantification.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, the findings support year-round exposure to eccentric hamstring strength, trunk-pelvis control, high-speed running mechanics, acceleration/deceleration, and football-specific actions. Building these capacities before injury makes rehab progressions more familiar and reduces the gap between rehab tasks and field demands.
rtp: RTP should be criteria-based and include objective strength, pain-free function, sprint/high-speed running tolerance, acceleration/COD actions, external workload comparison, and football-specific skill exposure. Imaging can inform prognosis, especially tendon involvement, but the survey shows it should not be the sole clearance criterion.

citation: O'Malley GR, Pema P, Sweeney P, Sarwar S, Cassimatis N, Knightly JJ, Goldstein I, Patel NV. Impact of Cervical and Lumbar Spine Surgeries on National Football League (NFL) Player Performance and Return-to-Play Outcomes. Cureus. 2025;17(6):e85706.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined NFL players who underwent cervical or lumbar spine surgery from 2005 to 2022 and evaluated return-to-play, career duration, and performance outcomes. Public injury reports and player data identified 144 spine surgeries among 136 players: 77 lumbar procedures across 71 players and 67 cervical procedures across 64-65 players. Lumbar surgery was more common in linemen and higher-BMI athletes, whereas cervical surgery was more common in skill positions. Lumbar procedures had a 61% return-to-play rate and average PFF performance rating increase of 6.3% among players with available ratings. Cervical procedures had a lower return-to-play rate reported as 47% in the discussion/abstract, with average PFF performance rating decrease of 5.8%. Cervical cases involved more fusions, and fewer cervical players improved their PFF rating compared with lumbar players.
tldr: NFL players returned more often and tended to perform better after lumbar spine surgery than after cervical spine surgery, highlighting the need for position- and region-specific rehab expectations.
methods: Retrospective observational cohort using publicly available NFL injury reports from 2005-2022; injury location, etiology, procedure type, age, height, weight, position, prior surgery, RTP, years played, Pro Bowl/Super Bowl data, and PFF performance ratings were collected from NFL.com, ESPN, FoxSports, CBS Sports, DraftSharks, Sharp Football Analysis, RotoWire, Pro-Football-Reference, and PFF; RTP was defined as appearing in another regular-season NFL game after surgery; analyses used Python, NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, and Statsmodels with t tests, chi-square/two-way ANOVA, linear regression for PFF change, and logistic regression for RTP.
findings:
- 144 spine surgeries were identified: 77 lumbar and 67 cervical.
- Lumbar surgery players had higher BMI than cervical surgery players (32.3 vs. 30.5; p=0.01303).
- Lumbar surgeries were concentrated among offensive and defensive linemen; cervical surgeries were more frequent in skill positions, especially RB, LB, and DB groups.
- Lumbar procedures were mostly discectomy/foraminotomy (75.3%), while cervical procedures had far more fusions (39.0% vs. 6.5%; p<0.00001).
- RTP occurred after 47/77 lumbar procedures (61.0%) and 31/67 cervical procedures; abstract/discussion summarize cervical RTP as 47%, while Table 1 lists 40.3%.
- Prior lumbar surgery was associated with higher RTP than no prior lumbar surgery (83% vs. 56%; p=0.02034).
- PFF data were available for 33 lumbar and 26 cervical cases; lumbar average PFF rose from 68 to 71 (+6.3%), while cervical average PFF fell from 68.1 to 66.7 (-5.8%).
- Fewer cervical players improved PFF rating than lumbar players (22% vs. 58%; p=0.01928).
limitations: Public injury reporting limited procedure specificity, exact anatomic detail, and medical context. PFF performance data were unavailable for many players and are less complete before/near PFF's early years. Selection bias is likely because only reported surgeries were captured. RTP was defined as one regular-season appearance, which does not equal full role restoration. The paper contains internal percentage inconsistencies for cervical RTP and a likely typo describing 47/77 lumbar returns as 77%.
practicalImplications: Lumbar and cervical spine surgeries should not be treated as one generic 'spine surgery' bucket. Linemen with lumbar pathology may have different loading histories, body-size constraints, and recovery expectations than skill players with cervical pathology. Post-op planning should align tissue healing, neurologic status, collision exposure, position demands, and realistic performance expectations.
athleteDev: For football development, this supports proactive trunk, hip, neck, and posterior-chain capacity work by position. Linemen need long-term lumbar load management and hip/trunk strength to tolerate blocking and load-bearing demands; skill players need cervical strength, tackling/contact technique, and exposure management because cervical injuries may be more performance-limiting.
rtp: RTP after spine surgery should be individualized by region, procedure, neurologic findings, position, and collision demands. One-game RTP rates are higher after lumbar than cervical surgery, but performance restoration is not guaranteed. Clearance should require medical/surgical healing, neurologic stability, progressive contact exposure, position-specific workload, and performance benchmarks rather than calendar time alone.
